steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; gallstone pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: The etiology of the pancreatitis (lipase 7580, amylase 1324) and transaminitis (ALT 455, AST 175, ALP 308, total bilirubin 2.4) is unclear — specifically, whether gallstones or biliary obstruction are the underlying cause, which would change management (e.g., ERCP, cholecystectomy timing).
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones in the gallbladder or common bile duct dilation (and possibly choledocholithiasis), pericholecystic fluid, or gallbladder wall thickening consistent with a biliary etiology for the pancreatitis and cholestatic liver enzyme pattern.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of markedly elevated lipase/amylase confirming acute pancreatitis, a cholestatic liver enzyme pattern (elevated ALP, total bilirubin, ALT > AST), and a prior similar RUQ pain episode strongly motivates ultrasound as the first-line imaging to identify gallstones or biliary obstruction as the precipitating cause.
grounding: HPI: RUQ pain radiating to back for the past two days with cramping and nausea
HPI: Had an episode of similar RUQ pain 3 months ago
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed acute pancreatitis (lipase ~126× upper limit of normal, amylase ~13× upper limit of normal) combined with a cholestatic liver injury pattern (elevated ALP, total bilirubin, and markedly elevated ALT/AST), which together raise strong suspicion for gallstone pancreatitis or choledocholithiasis. A prior similar RUQ episode three months ago further supports a recurrent biliary process. Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the appropriate first-line imaging to identify gallstones, assess the common bile duct for dilation, and evaluate the gallbladder for cholecystitis, as this will directly determine whether biliary intervention (ERCP) or early cholecystectomy planning is warranted.

HPI: Had an episode of similar RUQ pain 3 months ago
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed acute pancreatitis (lipase ~126× upper limit of normal, amylase ~13× upper limit of normal) combined with a cholestatic liver injury pattern (elevated ALP, total bilirubin, and markedly elevated ALT/AST), which together raise strong suspicion for gallstone pancreatitis or choledocholithiasis. verification.actual_finding: Several small gallstones in the gallbladder neck, mildly-to-moderately dilated intrahepatic ducts, and a common bile duct measuring 8 mm with the distal CBD not visualized due to bowel gas (stone not excluded).
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report directly supports a biliary etiology by demonstrating cholelithiasis and biliary ductal dilation consistent with the physician's expected finding of gallstones and possible biliary obstruction causing the pancreatitis and cholestatic liver enzyme pattern.
